title: C. difficile toxin: disrupts the actin cytoskeleton
vignette: A 65-year-old man comes to the office due to pain, redness, and swelling in his right calf. The patient is diagnosed with cellulitis and clindamycin is initiated. A few days after starting treatment, he develops watery diarrhea and abdominal cramps. The patient is hospitalized, and a complete blood count reveals leukocytosis. The toxin responsible for this patient's condition most directly affects which of the following components of intestinal mucosal cells?

clues.text: pain, redness, and swelling in his right calf
clues.note: This is just the setup - cellulitis that prompts antibiotics. The cellulitis itself is not the answer; the antibiotic that treats it is what precipitates the real problem.
Clindamycin is one of the classic antibiotics that disrupt colonic flora and allow Clostridioides difficile overgrowth. Broad-spectrum penicillins, cephalosporins, and fluoroquinolones do the same.
Profuse watery diarrhea with cramping a few days into antibiotics is the classic presentation of C. difficile colitis (pseudomembranous colitis).

C. difficile colitis often causes a marked leukocytosis (commonly >15,000/mm3; normal 4,500-11,000/mm3), reflecting the toxin-driven inflammatory response. Striking leukocytosis after antibiotics should trigger C. difficile testing.

whatMatters: Antibiotic (clindamycin) wipes out flora -> C. difficile overgrowth. Its toxins A and B inactivate Rho-family GTPases that maintain the ACTIN CYTOSKELETON, causing enterocyte rounding, tight-junction breakdown, fluid secretion (watery diarrhea), inflammation, and pseudomembranes. The toxin's most DIRECT target is cytoskeleton integrity. Answer C.
strategy: Map the toxin to its molecular target. C. difficile toxins A/B -> inactivate Rho -> disrupt ACTIN cytoskeleton (answer). Contrast with the buzzword targets: cholera toxin -> apical ion transport (cAMP/secretion), C. perfringens alpha toxin (lecithinase) -> cell membrane, Shiga/Shiga-like toxin -> 60S ribosome. 'Apical ion transport' is the cholera trap - C. difficile affects ion transport only INDIRECTLY via loss of cell polarity.

eliminations.label: Apical ion transport - cholera toxin (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: cholera toxin (Vibrio cholerae) ADP-ribosylates Gs, raising cAMP and driving Cl-/Na+ secretion through apical ion transporters -> profuse watery diarrhea. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: that is cholera's mechanism; C. difficile toxins act on the actin cytoskeleton, and ion transport is affected only SECONDARILY (loss of cell polarity). The trap is matching 'watery diarrhea' to the cholera mechanism. ✅ Correct if: rice-water stools from V. cholerae acting on apical secretion.

eliminations.label: Cell membrane integrity - C. perfringens alpha toxin
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: alpha toxin (a lecithinase/phospholipase) degrades membrane phospholipids, destroying cell membranes. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: that is the gas gangrene (myonecrosis) mechanism of C. perfringens, not C. difficile colitis. ✅ Correct if: clostridial myonecrosis after a contaminated penetrating wound.

eliminations.label: Mitochondrial energy production - cyanide/NRTIs
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: mitochondrial poisoning (cyanide blocks complex IV; some nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors are mitochondrially toxic). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: C. difficile toxins don't act on mitochondria; they disrupt the actin cytoskeleton. ✅ Correct if: a toxin/drug impairing oxidative phosphorylation.

eliminations.label: Ribosomal protein synthesis - Shiga/Shiga-like toxin
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: Shiga toxin (Shigella) and Shiga-like toxin (EHEC O157:H7) cleave the 60S ribosomal subunit, halting protein synthesis. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: those cause bloody diarrhea/HUS, not antibiotic-associated pseudomembranous colitis; C. difficile targets the cytoskeleton. ✅ Correct if: bloody diarrhea + HUS from a Shiga toxin.

reasoning: Clindamycin disrupts colonic flora -> C. difficile overgrowth (pseudomembranous colitis)
C. difficile produces toxins A and B that inactivate Rho-regulatory GTPases
Rho controls the actin cytoskeleton -> enterocyte rounding, tight-junction loss, fluid secretion, inflammation
-> the toxin most directly disrupts cytoskeleton integrity; answer C
mechanism: Antibiotics such as clindamycin kill commensal gut flora and allow pathogenic Clostridioides difficile to overgrow. C. difficile secretes toxin A and toxin B, which bind enterocytes, are internalized, and inactivate Rho-family regulatory GTPases that govern the ACTIN cytoskeleton. Loss of cytoskeletal integrity causes cell rounding/retraction, disruption of intercellular tight junctions, and increased paracellular fluid secretion (watery diarrhea); the toxins also stimulate neutrophilic inflammation and can induce apoptosis, producing the characteristic pseudomembranes. Apical ion transport is altered only indirectly (secondary to loss of cell polarity), which is why the most DIRECT target is the cytoskeleton rather than the apical transporters affected by cholera toxin.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Micro > C. difficile: antibiotic-associated (clindamycin, cephalosporins, fluoroquinolones, PPIs); toxins A & B inactivate Rho -> actin cytoskeleton disruption -> pseudomembranous colitis; treat with oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: The stem asks for the toxin's MOST DIRECT molecular target, not the clinical syndrome. C. difficile = actin cytoskeleton; don't pick the downstream/indirect effect.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on Rho GTPase, actin cytoskeleton, tight junctions, pseudomembrane, leukocytosis, or 'apical ion transport'? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Apical ion transport (your pick) is the CHOLERA toxin mechanism and is only indirectly affected here. 'Watery diarrhea' lures you toward cholera, but the direct C. difficile target is the cytoskeleton.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: C. difficile A/B -> Rho -> actin cytoskeleton; cholera -> apical ion transport (cAMP); C. perfringens alpha -> membrane; Shiga/Shiga-like -> 60S ribosome.

keywords.front: Watery diarrhea + leukocytosis days after clindamycin
C. difficile toxin A & B molecular action
keywords.back: Inactivate Rho GTPases -> disrupt actin cytoskeleton
Apical ion transport via Gs/cAMP (secretory diarrhea)
Oral fidaxomicin or oral vancomycin (IV metronidazole if fulminant)

firstAid.detail: Micro > antibiotic-associated pseudomembranous colitis; toxins A/B -> Rho/actin cytoskeleton; vancomycin/fidaxomicin.
firstAid.location: Micro > antibiotic-associated pseudomembranous colitis; toxins A/B -> Rho/actin cytoskeleton; vancomycin/fidaxomicin.

firstAid.detail: Micro > cholera (ion transport/cAMP), C. perfringens alpha (membrane), Shiga (60S ribosome).
firstAid.location: Micro > cholera (ion transport/cAMP), C. perfringens alpha (membrane), Shiga (60S ribosome).

firstAid.detail: Micro > clindamycin/cephalosporins/fluoroquinolones/PPI risk; stool PCR/EIA for toxin.
firstAid.location: Micro > clindamycin/cephalosporins/fluoroquinolones/PPI risk; stool PCR/EIA for toxin.

memoryHook: diff = the actin wrecker. C. difficile toxins cut the Rho/actin cytoskeleton (cells round up); only cholera goes after apical ion transport.
